---
title: Open Research Data Template
subtitle: A lean path from research data to website, citation, and archive
---

This template helps academics turn research data, documentation, code, and release notes into a public website and citable archive.

You do not need to learn every tool at once. Follow the checklist, preview the site while you write, and publish only after the project is understandable to a future reader.

## What You Get

| Output | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| GitHub repository | Data, code, licenses, citation metadata, issues, and project history. |
| Public website | A readable explanation for students, reviewers, collaborators, and data users. |
| Setup checklist | A clear path for replacing placeholders, validating files, and preparing releases. |
| Zenodo archive | Preserved releases with a DOI for citation. |

Open research data is easier to trust when the data, methods, code, documentation, and citation route stay together.

## Start Here

1. Create a new repository with [Use this template](https://github.com/new?template_name=open-research-data-template&template_owner=maehr).
2. Open the new repository in GitHub Codespaces or clone it locally.
3. Install dependencies and start the live preview.

```bash
npm install
npm run prepare
npm run preview
```

4. Work through `TODO.md` to replace placeholders and configure the project.
5. Edit `index.qmd` so the website explains your project to public readers.
6. Edit `README.template.md` so GitHub visitors understand the repository.

For most new projects, `TODO.md` is the safest next step. It tells you what to change and what can wait.

## Write Each Thing Once

| If you are writing... | Put it in... | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A short explanation of the repository and its folders | `README.md` | GitHub visitors need a quick front door. |
| A task that still needs to be done | `TODO.md` | Maintainers need one checklist for unfinished setup work. |
| A public explanation of the project, data, course, or publication | `index.qmd` | Website readers need the story, not maintenance notes. |
| Methods, data dictionaries, workflows, or detailed decisions | `documentation/` or `project-management/` | Details should be findable without making the homepage too long. |

This keeps the website readable, the repository navigable, and the setup work visible without mixing everything into one long page.

## Minimum Academic Checklist

Before publication, make sure a reader can answer these questions:

- What does the project document?
- Who created and maintains it?
- Which files contain the data, code, methods, and tests?
- What are the data sources, transformations, limitations, and licenses?
- How should the project be cited?
- Where can users report errors or ask reuse questions?

## Examples

The same structure can support research data documentation, reproducible workflows, teaching, and living publications:

- [Stadt.Geschichte.Basel RDM](https://dokumentation.stadtgeschichtebasel.ch/) for public history research data management.
- [sgb-figures](https://dokumentation.stadtgeschichtebasel.ch/sgb-figures/) for reproducible publication figures.
- [nordatlantisk-ft](http://mtwente.github.io/nordatlantisk-ft/) for voting records and workflow documentation.
- [Decoding Inequality 2025](https://dhbern.github.io/decoding-inequality-2025/) for a course website.
- [Non-discriminatory Metadata](https://maehr.github.io/diskriminierungsfreie-metadaten/) for a handbook and scholarly publication.

## Next Step

Create a repository from the template, keep `npm run preview` open while editing, and use `TODO.md` as the working checklist until the first release is archived.
